data.title: Markets Look Past Ice-Cold March CPI, Eyes On Tariffs
data.description:
The March CPI report was ice cold—consumer prices fell by the most in five years. The headline rate increased by just 0.05%, bringing the year-on-year pace to 2.39%, sharply below consensus estimates. The core CPI rate actually ticked down by 0.05% on a month-over-month basis, making for a year-on-year rate of 2.79%. Supercore prices fell in the final month of the first quarter.

Both the annual headline and core inflation rates are now the lowest since early 2021. Of course, this is now dated data in light of President Trump’s tariffs that are already in effect. Bank of America reported that March consumer spending ramped up ahead of the implementation of the increased duties.

Digging into the report, shelter prices jumped 4% in March, and food away from home rose 3.8%. Dragging on March CPI were gasoline, which fell by 9.8%, while new car prices were flat. Recent views of the Manheim Used Vehicle Value Index point to modest auto deflation, but that, too, won’t last given the steep auto tariffs that will seep in over the months ahead.

Core good CPI is now down 0.1% YoY, while Housing fell notably to 3.99% from 12 months ago. Key for the Fed, core services ex-housing is now just 2.91%--the softest since early 2021. That figure plunged 24 basis points in March.

A Very Tame March CPI Report

March Headline CPI Down in March, Core CPI Rate Inches Up Less Than Expected

Headline CPI +2.79% YoY, Core CPI +2.39% YoY

US Core CPI YoY: Lowest Since March 2021

Deflation in Supercore CPI

It was a muted market reaction to what was a very encouraging inflation update. S&P 500 futures (SPX) drifted lower, falling from a 1.7% loss to 2.0%. The Treasury market caught a bid, though. The yield on the benchmark 10-year Treasury note (US10Y) fell a few basis points but then drifted back to unchanged on the session, with particularly strong buying on the short end of the curve.

The US dollar (DXY) fell further after the 8:30 a.m. ET data dump, which included sanguine jobless claims figures for last week. The EURUSD (EUR:USD) pair moved up through the $1.11 figure—the highest since last September.

S&P 500 Futures Remained Red Post-CPI

The Dollar Is Now The Weakest Since Q3 2024

Treasury Yields Were Mixed, But Mainly Lower, After the CPI Report

The market now prices in a 16% chance of a May rate cut by the FOMC, and the odds of a June ease are high at 78%. Bigger picture, traders see a 55% chance of a U.S. recession this year, far below where that market traded before President Trump’s Truth Social post Wednesday afternoon. The S&P VIX Index (VIX) hangs high at 37.

Recession Chances Dropped This Week

Looking ahead, inflation data continues rolling in tomorrow. March PPI hits the tape in the premarket, while the first look at April University of Michigan consumer sentiment crosses the wires at 10 a.m. ET. The producer price report should be more impactful, as UMich remains riddled with political influence. Of course, any further announcements from the White House or Truth Social posts from President Trump will move markets, particularly ahead of the weekend.

Next week, the highlight is Retail Sales, along with Q1 earnings reports rolling in. Good Friday is an NYSE holiday, which will be a nice respite for investors given the incredibly volatile macro environment and stock market in the last week-plus.

Data Deck

The Bottom Line

It was a calm March CPI report, but markets paid little attention to it. We’ll likely get a rate cut in June, but all eyes are fixated on trade updates from the POTUS. Next week’s Retail Sales, if strong, should help keep Q1 GDP in the green. Amid high volatility, expect the roller coaster ride to continue.

data.title: Delta Air Lines: Stock Surges After Q1 Earnings Despite Guidance Drop
data.description:
In January, I covered Delta Air Lines (NYSE:DAL) with a buy rating. Unfortunately, the stock has lost nearly a third of its value since then. The obvious reason is the trade turmoil, which could eventually erode GDP growth. Before that happens, consumer confidence tanks and that affected bookings forcing Delta to lower its first quarter guidance.

Nevertheless, Delta Air Lines exceeded expectations sending the stock price higher and the decision of President Trump to postpone tariffs for most countries led to a further price recovery.

In this report, I will discuss the most recent earnings and update my price target for the stock.

Delta Air Lines Cabin Segmentation And Capacity And Revenue Management Drive Growth

Delta Air Lines grew revenues by 3.3% to nearly $13 billion in the first quarter. Passenger revenues increased 3% on a 4% increase in capacity. The domestic market saw the strongest unit revenue decline at 3%. Capacity growth of 4% resulted in a 1% increase in revenues. However, it is likely that Delta had initially anticipated stronger performance for the domestic market but the market turmoil eroded consumer confidence leading a pressure on bookings in the quarter as well as forward bookings.

In the Atlantic market, capacity declined by 3% which allowed the company to increase unit revenues by 8% for a revenue growth of 5%. That capacity seems to have been rotated into the Pacific market where there was a 16% increase in capacity on marginally higher unit revenues resulting in 16% growth in revenues. The Latin American market saw an 8% increase in capacity and 2% higher lower unit revenues for a 5% increase in sales.

I believe that absent of the market turmoil, we would have seen stronger unit revenues for the domestic and Latin American market. When there are macroeconomic shocks that affect consumer confidence, there is not a lot that Delta can do. However, the company managed the business rather well and especially the performance in the Atlantic and Pacific market showed that.

The company continued to see its premium product and loyalty program carry growth while main cabin revenues were 1% lower, which I believe also shows the challenging nature for airlines. Generally, main cabin is the most cost conscious cabin class and with market turmoil that is also where confidence gets eroded first and unit revenues get pressured subsequently.

Delta Air Lines Earnings Growth Outpaces Revenue Growth

Delta Air Lines increased total revenues by 2% and passenger revenues by 3% on a 4% increase in capacity. That is not the type of revenue increase we would like to see. The company had initially guided for 7% to 9% revenue growth, so we do quite clearly see how reduced consumer confidence affects the business.

Total expenses rose 3% to $13.5 billion driven by higher salaries and related costs, regional carrier costs, landing fees and profit sharing. The costs increased despite a reduction in fuel costs and costs related to the refinery business. Adjusted unit costs excluding fuel rose 2.6%

Delta Air Lines Removes Guidance For 2025

Initially, Delta Air Lines had guided for EPS to grow by more than 10% this year and free cash flow for more than $4 billion. The company has dropped its outlook for 2025 due to the macroeconomic uncertainties but does expect $1.5 billion to $2 billion in pre-tax income for the second quarter on flat revenues. Adjusted pre-tax income was $2 billion last year. So, essentially Delta is guiding lower at the midpoint and what seems to be the best case scenario now it will have flat pre-tax income.

That really tells the tale of how reduced consumer confidence has affected the first quarter results and is expected to affect second quarter results as well. At the time of writing a substantial portion of the tariffs (China excluded) has been postponed. That is good news, but it really does not tell us anything about how fast consumer confidence will return if it will and that is the macroeconomic reality that Delta has to navigate now.

Previously, the company saw strong demand for travel to Europe due to the strong dollar and with the trade turmoil the dollar has weakened and part of the appeal of traveling to Europe because your dollar is worth a lot has fallen of a cliff.

So, there is a lot of macroeconomic uncertainty that provide risk to Delta and airlines in general.

Earnings Estimates Tumble For Delta Air Lines

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener which uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and update the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

The market turmoil has had its impact on earnings and free cash flow estimates. For FY25, analysts are now expecting EBITDA of $8.6 billion compared to $10.4 billion earlier. This is a decrease of 16.7% and also for 2026 we see a trim to the earnings estimates of 10.3% to $9.7 billion. Essentially, 2025 EBITDA has shifted towards 2027. On free cash flow, the estimate has come down by 20% this year and 6.2% next year. So the estimates have been trimmed significantly.

The price target was $83.50 in an upbeat scenario and that has now decreased to $81.28 and given that there is a lot of uncertainty to the market I am trimming that further to $62.86. This is half of the 84% upside that is supported by the current estimates and reflects the uncertainty we see in the market today.

So I am maintaining my buy rating but I am trimming my price target from $73.56 to $62.86 and note that uncertainty remains and there is additional pressure on the estimates for the remainder of the year as we do not know how the current trade war will play out and at what timescale.

Conclusion: Delta Air Lines Feels Pain Of Trade War

What Delta Air Lines is feeling is the eroded consumer confidence that affected the company in the first quarter and it is unlikely that we will see a recovery this year. Analysts have also incorporated this in their estimates and I do believe that makes a lot of sense and there could even be additional downside.

However, at current price levels I do believe that substantial upside remains even when we discount the upside by half and as a result I am reiterating my buy rating but with a lower price target.

data.title: Super Micro Computer: The Shift You Can't Ignore
data.description:
Investment Thesis

While Super Micro Computer, Inc. (NASDAQ:SMCI), aka Supermicro, remains 15% below my last coverage, its performance closely mirrors the S&P 500's (SP500) 14% drawdown, a resilient showing for a high-beta AI infrastructure stock. Our earlier thesis, centered on governance risks, accurately anticipated the delayed 10-K resolution. With investor sentiment still cautious, the upcoming earnings may be the next true catalyst, leaving a valuable window for discerning investors to accumulate.

In today's dive, we assess two critical forces shaping SMCI's trajectory: the fragility of its pricing power in a rapidly commoditizing market and the durability of its moat as it evolves from server assembler to full-stack AI infrastructure platform.

Investors shouldn't be overly concerned with margin compression as long as gross margins remain stable around 11% and revenue continues to scale. This would validate our thesis that SMCI is transitioning into a high-volume, low-margin operating model optimized for scale and speed.

Pricing Power Or Race To The Bottom?

Supermicro's gross margin in the latest quarter hit just 11.8%, significantly below Hewlett Packard's (HPE) 29% and that of Dell's (DELL) at 25%. Even the EBITDA margin is behind HPE, leaving pricing power and sustainability of margin in doubt. The guilty party here is an effective combination of product as well as customer mix, margin-dilutive scale, as well as cost associated with its cutting-edge liquid cooling products.

The dramatic margin decline was not purely structural, CFO David Weigand states. Transitional costs due to next-gen Blackwell and Hopper platforms, ramping R&D spend, and skewed customer mix towards large hyperscalers impacted the quarter. These customers require custom configurations as well as deep volume pricing discounting. In essence, SMCI swapped volume for margin, as well as strategic position. Disruption due to the postponed filing of the 10-K was also reported as a contributor towards near-term volatility.

We forecast back in November that we would be down 100 basis points. And that was because of the customer mix and products that we saw shipping out.

Remember, we're working on more end-of-life products, which have become more competitive as customers wait for the new platforms...

We had some extra expenses as well, because we're spending more on R&D right now, and specifically in buying some of the advanced chips as we refine our engineering and production to get ready for what we consider will be very large shipments coming up.

An important component of this solution is SMCI's leadership in Direct Liquid Cooling (DLC). DLC is a cooling technology that utilizes liquid, not air, as the vehicle for pulling heat away from high-powered GPUs and CPUs within AI servers. It enables hardware to perform faster and more efficiently, particularly for high-power loads typical in AI training applications. DLC systems involve specialized components such as cold plates (which sit atop chips), coolant pumps, and specially crafted manifolds that distribute liquids across racks of servers.

While this configuration is highly effective at increasing compute density while reducing power usage per function, it is costly. Industry estimates suggest that DLC adds approximately 15-25% to the total server build cost compared to traditional air-cooled systems, driven by specialized components like cold plates, pumps, manifolds, and chillers. And since many components are acquired from a few specialized suppliers, some in Taiwan and some in China, pricing is unstable, and supply chain exposure is high.

Both HPE and Dell are following suit with different methodologies for their adoption of liquid cooling. Dell PowerEdge Systems employ rear-door heat exchanger units as well as CDU-based units, opting for hybrid designs that can seamlessly plug into typical data centers without the need for complete reconstruction. HPE gravitates toward tank-based and immersion cooling, which is more common in HPC clusters. SMCI's benefit is in vertical integration: it designs, manufactures, and deploys DLC racks in one facility. However, as HPE and Dell scale their DLC capabilities through refinement, SMCI's early mover advantage may fade.

Tariffs can compound this uncertainty. An impending tariff of between 50-100% on Chinese technology components, proposed in an eventual Trump administration, could double the price of DLC components and chassis frames if imported from China. Since more than 65% of SMCI procurement is linked with Supplier A, whose components are mostly imported, such tariffs would significantly inflate COGS.

Investors are also concerned about the concentration of revenue. One customer contributed 20% of net sales in FY 2024, while another contributed 44.8% of accounts receivables. Though unnamed, market sources indicate that leading Nvidia-powered (NVDA) cloud startup CoreWeave (CRWV) is a significant contributor. Such concentration is rare among hardware companies and illustrates the vulnerability of SMCI's prevailing revenue base.

Compare this with HPE and Dell. Dell's product mix is spread across consumer PCs, enterprise storage, and services, with AI servers representing only an increasing proportion of total revenue. Its greater size allows for superior procurement of components and a wider margin cushion. HPE, on the other hand, enjoys high-margin services (GreenLake) as well as long-term contracts for HPC with the government and research centers. Their revenue mix is one of stability and predictability that SMCI does not currently offer. To the contrary, nearly 95% of SMCI's revenue comes from the storage and server segment.

If it is not able to boost software and service layers or create stickier customer relationships, the danger is that its server products get commoditized. At ~12% gross margins, SMCI is already close to contract manufacturing levels rather than differentiated IT services. If price competition increases, particularly with Dell selling AI racks at close to breakeven in order to secure hyperscale deals, SMCI's margin might stabilize at the 10-11% level in the long term, closer to OEM assemblers rather than platform providers.

How Sustainable Is SMCI's Moat: From Server Assembler To AI Infrastructure Platform

Its differentiation is built on its modular Datacenter Building Block Solutions (DBBS), accelerated innovation cycles, and rack-scale liquid cooling capabilities. However, the sustainability of this moat is under scrutiny.

Recently, Supermicro has made it clear that it is moving away from being a "server assembler" toward an end-to-end data center infrastructure partner. During Q2 FY 2025, management announced expansion into end-to-end deployment, on-site cabling, and management software, functions that migrate toward service territory. These are indicators of intent to move toward being a platform provider rather than a product supplier.

Additionally, Dell no longer only makes laptops and office-oriented servers. Their new PowerEdge XE9680 systems are tuned especially for the needs of AI in this modern era. These servers now have their own brand of cooling in the form of liquids, as well as space in them for the latest Nvidia graphics processing units. Dell packages these servers with long-term service options, such as warranties, updates, and support agreements, which are appreciated by many enterprises. In plain words: Dell is making its AI servers enterprise-friendly and easier to purchase, install, and manage in the long run.

However, SMCI still enjoys a critical speed advantage in deployment. While Dell distributes via typical enterprise routes, SMCI's Silicon Valley fulfillment centers support rack-scale customization and delivery in weeks, not months. This responsiveness is determinative for quick-scaling customers such as xAI and CoreWeave.

More significantly, the coming Blackwell-based server system wave might fundamentally re-design the competitive landscape. Nvidia's GB200 NVL72 platform, with 36 Blackwell GPUs and 72 Grace CPUs, delivers 30x performance per watt vs. Hopper. Each rack will consume as much as 120kW of power, a level at which standard air cooling is no longer effective. This transition will expand TAM for DLC systems, in which today SMCI enjoys leadership. The company is forecasting DLC adoption at 30%+ of new data centers in 2025 vs. <10% in 2023.

This is not simply cosmetic in nature. Blackwell is the backbone of Nvidia's "AI factories" of full-stack supercomputing clusters for model training, inference, and real-time AI services. At GTC 2025, Nvidia emphasized partnerships with Dell, HPE, and Supermicro to bring these factories into customers' hands without directly naming exclusive hardware partners. Considering that SMCI shipped >70% of global DLC racks in 2024, it's very probable that SMCI will provide a significant share of Nvidia's AI factory installations, primarily where custom configurations and speed are paramount.

These infrastructure shifts support SMCI's claim that its opportunity isn't slowing down. The company is investing in a 1,500-rack-per-month DLC manufacturing capacity across the U.S. and Asia. Whether that capacity gets fully utilized will depend on execution, ecosystem trust, and competition.

Long-term, SMCI is still an AI-infrastructure high-beta play. Its upside is tied to platform transitions (Blackwell, sovereign AI, inference growth), but its downside is execution risk, supplier concentration, and margin volatility. Advanced investors should be looking at margin trends, service expansion, and diversification traction in order to assess whether SMCI is really able to move from speed-oriented disruptor to sustainable infrastructure leader.

However, in the absence of progression for SMCI above the level of hardware assembly, commoditization is an actual possibility. At 12.4% gross margins, 65% concentration of suppliers, and having the major customer in its accounts for close to half of its receivables, the business tilts perilously towards volume-driven, margin-lower dynamics. In such a case, the business increasingly leans toward a high-volume, low-margin operating model.

Takeaway

Supermicro stands at a pivotal crossroads. With AI infrastructure demand accelerating, its first-mover edge in liquid cooling and rapid deployment is real, but so are margin pressures and concentration risks. For savvy investors, the drop offers a chance to position ahead of a potential upside if execution matches ambition.

data.title: The Structural Bifurcation Will Drive Fidelis Insurance To Profitable Growth
data.description:
Investment Thesis

With the recent LA wildfires that occurred in January of 2025, property & casualty insurers have been on a roll over the last couple of weeks. One of the companies that was hit heavily was Mercury General Corporation (MCY), which I covered in my recent article. I also covered Skywest Specialty Insurance Group (SKWD), though the company was not much affected by the catastrophic losses of the wildfires. You can refer to the article here. While the insurance industry is a sensitive one, I would say there are lots of opportunities with specialty insurers that cover specific risks. Fidelis Insurance Group (FIHL) is one of these specialty insurance companies that I believe share the same similarities with Mercury and Skywest. Fidelis is trading at a forward P/E of 6.66x, which is a 31.14% discount to its sector median. Fidelis has solid growth prospects, with its forward diluted EPS expected to grow at 2.68% and revenue at 23.65%, a discount of 297.45% against its sector median. In my opinion, the company's 2025 prospects look promising, as noted by the CEO:

As we enter 2025, we are well-capitalized and positioned to drive profitable growth. Our underwriting talent, market access, financial strength and disciplined approach to capital management will continue to differentiate our business and enable us to deliver sustainable value for all our stakeholders

What I find impressive is that this company is executing a disciplined, diversified, and capital-efficient strategy. It has a strong capital base, a high CAGR rate of 36.1% of its GWP, and is rapidly expanding into new markets. As we had seen with Mercury, one of the major advantages of Fidelis is its strong diversification of insurance products, which eventually reduces risk in cases of catastrophic losses. I would also like to note that FIHL has a forward P/B of 0.61x against a sector median of 0.99x, a 38.22% discount. In my opinion, if the company can sustainably grow and maintain its book at 5-10% over the next couple of years, then this could turn out to be a great stock.

Business Overview

Fidelis has had a well-diversified business since 2015 when it was founded. It operates as a specialty insurance company with offices in London and Dublin. It offers a variety of over 100 insurance products across 10 business lines. As of December 2024, the company's Gross Written Premiums [GWP] stood at $4.4Bn, which is a 22% increase compared to 2023.

If you look at their GWP since 2018, FIHL has been growing its GWP by a CAGR of 36.1%, which I must say is quite attractive. Additionally, their combined ratio has been increasing over the years, while averaging 87.7%. While its combined ratio has been increasing, it is prudent to mention that Fidelis has had some adverse events in the aviation and aerospace segments, together with the impact of Russia-Ukraine aviation litigation, as noted by the CEO:

These results are inclusive of net adverse prior year period development in our Aviation and Aerospace line of business during the fourth quarter related to business underwritten in 2021 and 2022 (pre-bifurcation) and impacted by the ongoing Russia-Ukraine aviation litigation.

According to Swiss Re, the property & casualty market in the US is expected to experience a stable ROE of 10% in 2025. There is also an anticipated growth in premiums at 5%, as shown below. This coincides with what Fidelis management has guided on their 2025 performance.

The Structural Bifurcation Effect

In January 2023, Fidelis completed a structural bifurcation. What this meant was that Fidelis would split its operations into two entities with separate functions: Fidelis Insurance Group and The Fidelis Partnership (MGU). In my opinion, this split has had many advantages to date, including a clear separation of risk and capital.

While Fidelis Insurance Group was primarily tasked with managing risk, capital, and returns, MGU would source underwriting business. While working with other institutions such as Blackstone (BX) and Travelers (TRV), MGU's role was to provide access to top-tier underwriting business with zero to minimal costs. As far as I am concerned, this enabled capital growth. As of today, the partnership has expanded to other global players such as Lloyds (LYG) of London. Other companies that have used this model are Arch Capital (ACGL) and RenaissanceRe (RNR) which have seen strong investor support.

Valuation

In terms of historical P/B, Fidelis has been growing its book value

per share at an average of 37% y/y. What I find impressive is that Fidelis is already trading at a 44.6% discount from its book value per share. In my opinion, there is a definite, hidden value in the stock that the market has not factored in yet.

As mentioned earlier, FIHL trades at a discount against its sector median from a forward P/E and P/B perspective, which reinforces my thesis that the stock could be deeply undervalued, as shown below.

In regard to its peers, it is evident that Fidelis is trading at a lower P/B multiple compared to its peers. While Fidelis trades at 0.69x, Roots (ROOT) trades at 8.01x, Steward Information (STC) trades at 1.27x, HCI Group (HCI) trades at 3.27x, while Hamilton Insurance (HG) and Trupanion (TRUP) trade at 0.78x and 4.33x, respectively.

Fidelity Key Risks

The biggest risk I see for Fidelis is exposure to catastrophic losses. Fidelis is heavily exposed to reinsurance property catastrophic rates. As we saw with Mercury and Skywest, catastrophic events could be a huge nuisance to a company's long-term growth and profitability. With the huge competition and surge of P&C insurers venturing into this market, catastrophic rates have been declining over the years. However, with the recent catastrophic wildfires, tariffs, rising costs, climate change, and recent hurricanes, there are chances that catastrophic rates will go up. The California Department of Insurance (DOI) is already looking into rate modification, and so will other states and countries.

Final Thoughts

The structural bifurcation has definitely had a positive effect on the overall performance of Fidelis Insurance Group so far. Fidelis exhibits a lot of the characteristics I saw with Skyward Specialty Insurance. A relatively small insurance company. Efficient management. A niche target market and a diversified pool of products. What's interesting with Fidelis is that after the bifurcation, the company has been able to attract more top-tier partners, and this could put FIHL in the big leagues. It may definitely take a while for the company to realize its true value, especially given the fact that the company is still paying claims from old policies and the Russia-Ukraine aviation litigation. However, I still believe that with its consistent growth prospects in GWP, the true value of Fidelis Insurance Group is yet to come.

MANAGER COMMENTARY

As of 12/31/2024

“We are conscious of valuations becoming elevated and aware of risks arising from unexpected outcomes in global elections or geopolitics, but our base case is for

continued economic growth with easing global financial conditions.”

Harbor Multi-Assets Solutions Team

Market in Review

The fourth quarter of 2024 delivered mixed investment results across risk assets, with investor focus squarely centered on the U.S. elections and resulting policy as the prevailing driver of asset returns. U.S. equities added to gains from previous quarters as the S&P 500 Index returned 2.4% during the fourth quarter, extending year-to-date gains to 25%. Similarly, the Russell 1000® Index returned 2.8%. Small caps continued to lag their larger peers with the Russell 2000® Index returning 0.3% for the quarter and 11.5% year to date. U.S. equities were supported by expectations that Trump’s economic policies would increase economic growth, lower taxes, and reduce regulation. Within the U.S. market, momentum continued as growth equities continued to outperform value peers. The Information Technology and Consumer Discretionary sectors also performed strongly. International equities lagged U.S. peers as the prospect of Trump’s proposed policy to increase tariffs on goods imported to the United States weighed on shares. Fixed-income markets experienced elevated volatility during the quarter, driven by increased geopolitical tensions, fluctuating inflation expectations, and changes in central bank policies. U.S. Treasuries sold off with concerns about an inflationary Trump policy agenda and sticky inflationary measures, leading markets to anticipate fewer rate cuts in 2025 and bond yields to rise in sympathy. The U.S. Federal Reserve (“Fed”) cut rates for the third consecutive meeting, but Fed Chair Powell acknowledged there may be fewer-than-expected cuts ahead due to inflationary pressures. In Europe, central banks continued to cut rates, but fears of government instability in France and budgetary concerns in the United Kingdom dampened bond returns during the quarter.

Portfolio Performance

During the fourth quarter, the Harbor AlphaEdge Small Cap Earners ETF (“ETF”) returned –1.85% (NAV), outperforming the Harbor AlphaEdge Small Cap Earners Index, which returned –2.07%, and underperforming its benchmark, the Russell 2000® Index, which returned 0.33%.

The ETF underperformed the benchmark, as investor enthusiasm post-election caused a risk-on rally, where lower-quality securities were rewarded and profitability de-emphasized for investors. The Health Care sector delivered positive relative performance versus the benchmark, particularly in the biotechnology industry. Our material underweight allocation to Health Care was a contributing factor, while the Health Care sector in the benchmark underperformed due to concerns about the administration’s potential policy changes having a negative impact on profitability.

Our underweight within the Information Technology sector was a drag on relative performance, as Information Technology was the leading sector within the Russell 2000® Index during the quarter.

Outlook

Our business cycle regime indicator continues to signal late-cycle economic conditions with market sentiment exhibiting risk-seeking behavior. Economic growth remains resilient in the U.S., as third quarter Gross Domestic Product growth came in at a healthy 3.1% on the back of strength in consumer spending. In the U.S., the incoming administration has signaled its desire to implement pro-growth policies such as tax relief and deregulation. Outside of the U.S., the incoming administration’s focus on tariffs will keep expectations for global growth under pressure due to uncertainty on trade policy. Central banks around the world continued to cut interest rates in response to increased confidence from authorities that we are well past peak inflationary pressures. The Fed cut rates by 25 basis points in consecutive meetings during the quarter. This was viewed as a hawkish cut, which tempered market expectations for the potential number of additional cuts in 2025. As our base case, we expect risk assets to experience continued positive returns in the coming quarters, with economic growth remaining resilient, but we are focusing on how new policy measures may affect the inflationary outlook and central bank policy going forward. We are conscious of valuations becoming elevated and aware of risks arising from unexpected outcomes in global elections or geopolitics, but our base case is for continued economic growth with easing global financial conditions.

Performance data shown represents past performance and is no guarantee of future results. Past performance is net of management fees and expenses and reflects reinvested dividends and distributions. Investment returns and principal value will fluctuate and when redeemed may be worth more or less than their original cost. Returns for periods less than one year are not annualized. Current performance may be higher or lower and is available through the most recent month end at harborcapital.com or by calling 800-422-1050.

Shares are bought and sold at market price not net asset value (NAV). A fund’s NAV is the sum of all its assets less any liabilities, divided by the number of shares outstanding. Market price returns are based upon the closing composite market price and do not represent the returns you would receive if you traded shares at other times.

IMPORTANT INFORMATION

Risks

Investing involves risk, principal loss is possible. Unlike mutual funds, ETFs may trade at a premium or discount to their net asset value. Harbor ETFs are new and have limited operating history to judge.

All investments involve risk including the possible loss of principal. There is no guarantee that the investment objective of the Fund will be achieved. Stock markets are volatile and equity values can decline significantly in response to adverse issuer, political, regulatory, market and economic conditions. Stocks of small cap companies pose special risks, including possible illiquidity and greater price volatility than stocks of larger, more established companies. The Fund may not exactly track the performance of the Index with perfect accuracy at all times. Tracking error may occur because of pricing differences, timing and costs incurred by the Fund or during times of heightened market volatility. Harbor Capital Advisors, Inc. (the “Index Provider”) selects companies for the Index based on a proprietary methodology and there is no guarantee that the construction methodology will accurately provide the intended exposure. The Fund's assets may be concentrated in a particular sector or industries to the extent the Index is concentrated, which would subject the Fund to the risk that economic, political, or other market conditions that have a negative effect on that sector or industry will negatively impact the value of the Fund. There can be no assurance that the Fund will grow to or maintain an economically viable size, in which case the Board of Trustees may determine to liquidate the Fund.

Benchmarks

The Harbor AlphaEdge Small Cap Earners Index evaluates the performance of a specific subset within the small cap US Equity market. This subset comprises companies listed in the Russell 2000 Index that have met profitability criteria over the past twelve quarters. The index employs a modified profitability weighting methodology.

The Russell 2000® Index measures the performance of the small-cap segment of the US equity universe. It is a subset of the Russell 3000® and includes approximately 2000 of the smallest securities based on a combination of their market cap and current index membership. The Russell 2000® Index and Russell® are trademarks of Frank Russell Company.

The constituents of the Russell 2000® Index were used by Harbor Capital Advisors, Inc. or its affiliate as the starting universe for selection of the companies included in the Harbor AlphaEdge Small Cap Earners Index. Frank Russell Company (“FTSE Russell”) does not in any way create, calculate, maintain, review, sponsor, support, promote or endorse the Harbor AlphaEdge Small Cap Earners Index. In no event shall any FTSE Russell party have any liability for any direct, indirect, special, incidental, punitive, consequential (including without limitation lost profits) or any other damages in connection with the constituents of the Harbor AlphaEdge Small Cap Earners Index.

Disclosures

Expense ratio information is as of the Fund's current prospectus, as supplemented. Gross expenses are the Fund's total annual operating expense.

Due to the security valuation procedures of the Fund and intra-day trading activity not included in the FactSet calculations, the actual returns may vary. From time to time the cash return in the portfolio may appear distorted based on the way FactSet’s attribution calculation methodology addresses delayed settlements.

Best and Worst Performers sections reflect stocks in the portfolio for the quarter with an average weight of 0.25% or greater.

Views expressed herein are drawn from commentary provided to Harbor by the subadvisor and may not be reflective of their current opinions or future actions, are subject to change without prior notice, and should not be considered investment advice.

This information should not be considered as a recommendation to purchase or sell a particular security. The weightings, holdings, industries, sectors, countries, and returns mentioned may change at any time and may not represent current or future investments.

As a result of changing market conditions, total net asset levels, expenses and other statistics may change at any time and may differ from those shown.

The total amount shown for sector, industries, or country holdings may be greater than 100% because of the inclusion of derivatives and the collateral securities supporting those instruments. Sector allocations are determined using the Global Industry Classification Standard (GICS), which is a service of Morgan Stanley Capital International (MSCI) and Standard & Poor's (S&P).

Investors should carefully consider the investment objectives, risks, charges and expenses of a fund before investing. To obtain a summary prospectus or prospectus for this and other information, visit harborcapital.com or call 800-422-1050. Read it carefully before investing.

Foreside Fund Services, LLC is the Distributor of the Harbor ETFs.

Attribution Disclosures

Linked Performance by Sectors data is produced from FactSet using data supplied by State Street Bank and Trust Company. Active Currency Contribution is the Currency Contribution of the portfolio minus the Currency Contribution of the benchmark.

Allocation Effect is the portion of portfolio excess return that is attributable to taking different group bets from the benchmark. (If either the portfolio or the benchmark has no position in a given group, allocation effect is the lone effect.) A group's allocation effect equals the average percent capitalization of the portfolio's group minus the average percent cap of the benchmark's group times the total return of the benchmark group minus the total return of the benchmark.

Average Weight is the dollar value (price times the shares held) of the security or group, divided by the total dollar value of the entire portfolio displayed as a percentage. It is calculated as the simple arithmetic average of daily values.

Contribution to Return is the contribution of a security or group to the overall portfolio return. It is calculated as the security weight multiplied by the daily security return linked daily across the reporting period.

Currency Contribution is Total Return in USD subtracting out the Local Returns.

Local Returns are the Total Return of the portfolio or benchmark using the local currency.

Selection Effect is the portion of portfolio excess return attributable to choosing different securities within groups from the benchmark. A group's security selection effect equals the average weight of the benchmark's group times the total return of the portfolio's group minus the total return of the benchmark's group.

Total Effect is the sum of Allocation Effect and Selection Effect. The total effect represents the opportunity cost of what was done in a group relative to the overall portfolio. It is not just the difference between percent contribution in the portfolio and benchmark. At the overall portfolio level, the two numbers are equal. At the group level, they can be different.

Total Return is the price change of a security or group including dividends accrued over the report period (or the in-portfolio return) which includes only the time period that each security was in the portfolio.

Definitions

Median Market Cap: The median size of the companies in a portfolio or index as measured by the market value of outstanding shares.

Weighted Average Market Capitalization: The average size of the companies in a portfolio or index as measured by the market value of outstanding shares. Earnings per share (EPS) is the portion of a company's profit allocated to each outstanding share.

The Est 3-5 Yr EPS Growth (%) is the estimated growth of earnings per share over the next 3-5 years, using pre-calculated mean long-term EPS growth rate estimates, which are calculated using each individual

broker’s methodology, from FactSet, First Call, I/B/E/S Consensus, and Reuters. Forward looking estimates may not come to pass.

% EPS Growth - Past 3 Year: Earnings per share refers to the bottom-line measure of a company’s profitability defined as net income divided by the number of outstanding shares. The Adjusted Trailing P/E (price-to-earnings) Ratio is the closing stock price divided by the sum of the last 12 months actual EPS.

Alpha is a measure of risk (beta) adjusted return.

Forecasted P/E Ratio: a measure of the P/E (price-to-earnings) ratio using forecasted earnings for the P/E calculation. Return on Equity (ROE) is a measure of financial performance calculated by dividing net income by shareholders' equity. The Price/Book (price-to-book) Ratio evaluates a firm's market value relative to its book value.

All P/E, ROE and P/B statistics are calculated as weighted medians.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: CureVac N.V. 2024 Q4 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by CureVac N.V. in conjunction with their 2024 Q4 earnings call.

data.title: Broadcom: The Trade War's Next Big Loser
data.description:
Broadcom (NASDAQ:AVGO) was able to grow its sales at a decent rate in recent years thanks to the ongoing generative AI revolution. However, as macro and geopolitical risks are piling up at a time when its stock has entered the overbought territory and could be considered overvalued, we believe that now is the right time for investors to dump Broadcom’s shares.

Time to Sell Broadcom

The recent escalation of the global trade war by the Trump administration is bad news for Broadcom. With a forward P/E of 23x, its stock is already overvalued in comparison to the sector median of 18x. While in certain situations higher multiples are justifiable, that’s not the case for Broadcom in our opinion. Even stocks of companies like AMD (AMD) and Nvidia (NVDA), which we also believe are uninvestable in the current environment, trade at lower multiples while their underlying businesses have better earnings growth rate expectations for the current fiscal year. This makes them better positioned to face the rising macro risks in comparison to Broadcom.

Broadcom’s growth thesis is also under threat of being undermined. Although the company is bullish about the ability to generate around $60 billion to $90 billion from its three major customers in FY27 due to the rising demand for accelerators and has upped its R&D investments, the trade war might crush the company’s hopes. FY27 is still far away from now, and the changing macro environment might lead to a decrease in demand over time.

Broadcom is already greatly exposed to Apple (AAPL), which helps the company generate around 20% of revenues. The worsening of the Sino-American relations along with the higher trade barriers might crush Apple’s performance and make the company decrease its orders, which could negatively affect Broadcom’s sales in the foreseeable future.

In addition to that, Apple is also planning to release its custom Wi-Fi chip this year, which might replace Broadcom’s offering. This could be a big deal for Broadcom as well since Apple accounts for around 65% of revenues for its RF chip segment.

Broadcom’s non-Apple business is also at risk of disruption. Around 20% of Broadcom’s revenues are generated in the Chinese market, which is an issue in the current macro environment. The company is already about to lose between $2 billion and $3 billion as a result of the decision of ByteDance to cancel its order with Broadcom to design the custom AI accelerator.

The latest implementation of reciprocal tariffs by the United States and the retaliatory tariffs by China might not only negatively affect the company’s performance due to the potential recession but also disrupt its Asia-oriented supply chain. With semiconductor tariffs on the horizon, we don’t see an upside for Broadcom’s stock at this stage.

The company already lost 30% of its value since the start of the year, and the potential materialization of risks described above might prevent a rebound in share price and its return to all-time high levels at which the company was trading only a few months ago.

Risks To Our Bearish Thesis

The good thing about Broadcom is that it has a strong foothold in the XPU market and XPU chips might become an alternative to expensive Nvidia GPUs that currently dominate the broader AI accelerator market. If that’s the case, then Broadcom indeed could offset the tariff-related losses over time by scaling the production and sales of its chips.

At the same time, 60% of VMware’s customers have moved to the subscription model, which already helped Broadcom nearly double its infrastructure software revenues last quarter. In the future, this could help the company generate more stable and predictable cash flows and decrease the exposure of the company to the cyclicality of the semiconductor business.

In addition to that, Broadcom's potential strengthening of relations with TSMC (TSM) as a result of the formation of a joint venture could allow it to better weather supply disruptions in comparison to its peers. Also, if geopolitical risks ease or Broadcom manages to find successful workarounds to decrease costs, then the negative impact from tariffs and export controls could be reduced as well.

The Real Value of Broadcom

To determine Broadcom’s intrinsic value, we created a valuation model. The valuation model was created when Broadcom was trading at $160.36 per share. The perpetual growth rate in the model is 3%, which is a rate that we use most of the time when creating valuation models for other businesses. Our projection covers the next five years, and the data like cash and long-term debt was taken from the most recent earnings report.

The discount rate in the model is 7.71%. We calculated it by figuring out Broadcom’s cost of equity and cost of debt. For the cost of debt calculation, we mostly used its TTM numbers. For the cost of equity calculation, we used a risk-free rate of 4.20%, a beta of 1.07, and a market return rate of 7.77%.

Our sales forecast is largely similar to the current consensus. The EBIT margin is unchanged as there’s a risk that the trade war will prevent the expansion of margins and negatively affect the bottom-line performance. The tax rate is 15%, which might become a new corporate tax rate in the United States in the future. The assumptions for the change in NWC are 1% of sales, which is the same rate we used when valuing other semiconductor names. The rest of the assumptions in the model are similar to Broadcom’s historical performance.

Based on those assumptions, the valuation model shows that Broadcom’s enterprise value is $547.52 billion. That translates to an equity value of $490 billion and an intrinsic value of $104.59 per share, which indicates that Broadcom is overvalued by around 35% right now.

Final Thoughts

While Broadcom is a decent company that has performed well in recent years, the rising macro risks are making us believe that its stock is a SELL at the current price. The company’s exposure to Apple, China, and the global supply chain is a bad thing in the current environment where great powers are implementing protectionist policies that will likely result in a recession in the not-so-distant future.

data.title: Dividend Harvesting Portfolio Week 214: $21,400 Allocated, $2,152.32 In Projected Dividends
data.description:
Liberation Day turned into market destruction day as the announcement of reciprocal tariffs caused the S&P 500 to decline by -7.9% while the Nasdaq fell by -7.94% this week. All the gains from the past year have been erased, and the S&P 500 is approaching bear market territory as it's fallen by 17.46% since making new all-time highs in February. Fed Chair Powell spoke on Friday and stood his ground on rate cuts while indicating that the impact from tariffs could be larger than initially projected. The only change that I recognized was that he indicated that the Fed would need to balance fighting inflation with protecting the labor market. We got the U.S. non-farm payrolls on Friday, and the U.S. economy added 228,000 jobs in March, which was well above the consensus forecast of 135,000. In my opinion, the market is overreacting as there simply isn't enough data to support the rapid descent in equities. Nothing is finalized, and for all we know, countries could be negotiating behind the scenes, and we could get an onslaught of new data over the next several days. I am more concerned about what the CEOs will say during the Q1 earnings calls and if guidance will remain the same for 2025 or be lowered. A lot can happen in the next several weeks, and I just think there isn't enough data to predict accurately how companies will be impacted. If tariffs go into effect without negotiations, then the market could be in for a rough 2025, but there isn't enough information to make that determination, and the selloff is just a knee-jerk reaction at this point. I am buying into weakness, and for the time being, I am not worried about tariffs.

The markets sent every portfolio into a downward spiral, even the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio. After allocating $21,400 to the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio, the account balance finished in week 214 at $24,808.46. This was the largest weekly loss as the account's profitability decreased by -$1,953.93 to $3,408.46, which is a return on invested capital of 15.93%. After declining by -9.25%, I continued to sleep like a baby, and I am looking at the recent price action as an opportunity to buy. This week, I added to my positions in BP p.l.c. (BP), and the BlackRock Science and Technology Term Trust (BSTZ). The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio generated $105.28 of dividend income in week 214. The combination of reinvesting the generated dividend income and making these investments increased the forward projected annualized dividend income by $23.60 (1.11%) to $2,152.32. The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio has generated $3,791.52 of income since inception, which is 17.72% of the total capital I have allocated toward the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio. While this week wasn't fun for many investors, the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio has an 8.68% yield on the current asset value and a 10.06% yield on cost. I am ready for choppy waters and still have a significant cushion between profitability and allocated capital. I am looking forward to generating recurring income, which will be immediately reinvested while adding to positions I feel are deeply discounted. Regardless if it takes 2 weeks or 1 year for the market to rebound, I will be allocating capital each week and building out the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio.

The Overall Performance of the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio

This was a brutal week for the investment community, and the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio wasn't spared. Nothing worked this week, and even positions that have limited tariff exposure, such as SL Green Realty (SLG), fell by -10.75%. Volatility is part of investing, and I am confident in the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio's strategy. I wish I would allow myself to invest more than $100 per week at times like this because I see so many opportunities, but I have to stay within the original framework of the portfolio and replicate investing on a set budget. This week, the account's profitability fell by -9.25%, and this was the largest weekly drop on both a dollar and percentage basis. I do believe that the strategy will prove to work well over the coming weeks as the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio will continue to generate recurring income, which will be reinvested, and I will be adding to positions, no matter what happens. I am still up 15.93% on invested capital and generating a portfolio yield of 8.68%, which will help me ride out of the storm.

The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio Dividend Section

Here's how much dividend income is generated per investment basket:

  • ETFs $646.77 (30.05%)
  • Equities $520.68 (24.19%)
  • CEFs $385.41 (17.91%)
  • REITs $361.51 (16.80%)
  • BDCs $237.96 (11.06%)

Collecting dividends can serve many functions in a portfolio. Some investors utilize dividends to supplement their income and live off of them. I'm building a dividend portfolio for myself 30 years into the future. In 2022, I collected $507.80 in dividend income from 533 dividends. In 2023, I collected $978.11 in dividend income from 660 dividends. 2024 was a great year as the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio generated $1,623.14 in dividend income from 709 dividends. After 14 weeks into 2025, $613.94 of dividend income was produced, which is 37.82% of the total income from 2024. There were 205 positions that paid a dividend, which is 28.91% of the dividends generated in 2024.

March is finished, and a new record has been established, as there was $105.28 of dividend income generated in week 214. This is almost double week 14 in 2024, as $54.04 was generated. This is my first triple-digit week, and it helped drive a lot of numbers higher. My weekly dividend income average in 2025 is $43.85, which is an increase of $12.64 (40.49%) YoY. It's going to be interesting to see if this will be the largest amount of weekly dividend income generated, or if I can get close to the $125 level before 2025 is over. I think there is a lot of potential, and the dividend snowball continues to grow as more income is generated each and every week.

Q1 is officially over, and my new projections are very bullish when it comes to income generation. In Q1 2024, the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio generated $321.80. This increased by 66.45% ($213.83) in Q1 2025 to $535.63. March ended up being a very strong month as $189.26 of dividend income was produced. If I assume that I will produce 66.45% more dividend income in 2025 than I did in 2024, the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio will generate $2,701.59 in dividend income, which is significantly more than my original assumptions. I am very interested to see how Q2 plays out as the dividend income generated gradually increases throughout the year during 2024. While the market crashed this week, the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio was generating recurring income, and I am excited about all the future progress that is being made.

The market performance this week has helped some positions get into the green section of the table below, which represents all the positions that are generating at least 1 share from their dividends. There are now 44 positions accomplishing this, and the additional shares are projected to produce an additional $245.82 in dividend income. While falling markets are never fun, I am excited about buying into weakness as I see a lot of deals on the horizon. I am going to do what I can to allocate capital toward positions that are outside of the green section while equities are on sale.

The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio Composition

REITs are still the largest segment in the portfolio, and I believe this will be the story for a while. Individual equities now represent 38.48% of the Dividend Harvesting portfolio while generating 24.19% of the dividend income. REITs, ETFs, CEFs, and BDCs make up 61.52% of the portfolio and generate 75.81% of the forward income. I am working on getting individual equities to represent more of the portfolio. The long-term goal is to have the sectors be close to equal weight, but that may not happen for 5–10 years at this rate, if ever. I think there are a lot of opportunities in energy and financials, so don't be surprised if I am adding to positions in these segments over the next several weeks.

The top-10 holdings are starting to look out of sync again, but I think this is going to end up being an anomaly. I was having a conversation with someone about Altria Group (MO) this week, and it was incredible how low shares were last year. I haven't added to MO since February 2025, and it's still 4.85% of the portfolio. I think that over 2025, I will get the top-20 positions to even out further, and I would like to see MO and Realty Income (O) represent less than 4% of the portfolio heading into the end of the year.

The NEOS NASDAQ-100(R) High Income ETF (QQQI) is no longer part of the top-10 holdings, and for the first time, British American Tobacco (BTI) is making an appearance. This has changed the dynamics of the top 10 a bit, and several positions have paid their dividends this week. My allocation to the top 10 positions is $6,129.83, and they finished the week with a value of $7,690.36. This is an ROI of $1,560.53 (25.46%). There has been $1,072.79 of dividends generated and reinvested, which is 17.50% of the initial investment into these positions. I'm now projecting that $585.81 of dividend income will be generated from the top 10 holdings, which have a forward yield of 9.56%. These positions represent 31% of the portfolio, while their forward dividend income is projected to generate 27.22% of the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio's annualized income.

Week 214 Additions

In week 214 I added to my positions in BP p.l.c., and the BlackRock Science and Technology Term Trust.

BP

  • Energy took a hit this week, and BP fell -15.91%, pushing its dividend yield to 6.61%
  • I think we are going to need more energy across all forms to support the data center build-out that is occurring
  • BP has always traded at a discount to the other supermajors, and I think that the current price is a drastic discount from where it should trade
  • Regardless of what happens with tariffs, I am excited to add to BP under $30 as they are generating $12 billion in free cash flow (FCF), buying back stock, and paying a large dividend

BSTZ

  • I wanted to get BSTZ to generate at least 1 share per year, so I added enough shares this week to make that happen
  • BSTZ has a large allocation to the Magnificent Seven, and with the amount of value that was lost in the market, I wanted to add to a position that had exposure to big tech
  • I think that the market will be higher a year from now than it is today, so getting BSTZ in a deep correction was very exciting to me
  • BSTZ now has a dividend yield of almost 16%, and I am looking forward to all of the income being reinvested

Week 215 Game Plan

Next week, I am probably going to allocate capital toward BP again, Kinder Morgan (KMI), and the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD).

Conclusion

This was a difficult week in the markets, but the dividend income rolling in certainly softened the blow. The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio is still in the black and is projected to generate $2,152.32 of dividend income for the remainder of the year if I don't allocate another dollar. Now that Q1 is finished, I have updated my projections, and I believe that I was very conservative with my original estimates, and there is the potential to exceed $2,700 of dividend income by the end of the year. I am not worried about tariffs yet, as I don't have enough information to make an accurate assessment. I want to see how the next several weeks play out and what CEOs are saying on their earnings calls. I am excited to allocate capital into a down market and look forward to seeing how this story plays out. Please leave all of your suggestions below, as I try to respond to everyone in the comment section.

data.title: GreensKeeper Q1 2025 Portfolio Update
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the GreensKeeper Value Fund Q1 2025 Letter.

Top Contributors

  • The top contributor to the portfolio in Q1 was Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A, BRK.B) with a return of +17.5%. Berkshire finished 2024 on a strong note, with operating earnings rising 27.1% from the prior year. The Insurance and Utilities & Energy segments showed stark improvement, with profits increasing 66% and 60% respectively year-over-year. With over $300 billion of idle cash at year-end, Buffett may finally be getting his opportunity to bag an elephant. Like his major acquisitions of the past, these moves drive the future growth of the company’s cash generation machine.
  • Vertex Pharmaceuticals (VRTX) was our second-best performer in the quarter, +20.4%. Growing demand for its Cystic Fibrosis ('CF') franchise continued to generate substantial free cash flow. Vertex remains focused on diversifying its product portfolio and recently launched two new approved therapies: Alyftrek and Journavx. Alyfrtek is an improvement to VRTX’s CF portfolio, offering a once-daily dosing regimen that is far more convenient for patients. Importantly, it covers 31 additional rare mutations of CF for which no previous treatment existed. Journavx, a non-opioid pain signal inhibitor for acute pain, is beginning to ramp up sales as physicians gain confidence in its real-world efficacy and as VRTX advances reimbursement negotiations with payors. We believe that Journavx has the potential to become a blockbuster therapy, providing significant quality-of-life improvements to patients. In addition, VRTX is developing therapies for chronic pain that utilize a similar mechanism of action—blocking selective sodium channels—to inhibit the transmission of pain signals with little off-target effects. Success would be great news for shareholders, but more importantly, offer a powerful alternative to opioids and help combat the opioid crisis.
  • Our third top performer in the quarter was Check Point Software Technologies (CHKP) +22.1%. CHKP’s enterprise cybersecurity solutions continue to gain traction in the market, supported by growing demand for advanced network security. Recently appointed CEO Nadav Zafrir, a pioneer in the Israeli cyber-security market, has bolstered the company’s executive sales force in efforts to accelerate growth. CHKP is well-capitalized, with over $2.5 billion of excess cash on the balance sheet, and continues to actively repurchase its own shares.
  • Rounding out our top 5 performers in Q1 were Elevance Health (ELV) +17.9% and Intercontinental Exchange (ICE) + 15.8%. As mentioned in the last Scorecard, we believe the sell-off in ELV over the past year has been overdone, and the stock is trading at a significant discount to our estimate of its intrinsic value. The recent rebound reflects a partial correction of that mispricing. ICE continues to perform well as a significant portion of its earnings is driven by transaction volume on its exchanges and increasing demand for financial data, both of which generally increase when panic-induced volatility hits the markets.

Top Detractors

  • Our biggest laggard in the first quarter was Alphabet Inc. (GOOG, GOOGL) -18.3%. Shares sold off following the company’s year-end earnings release in February, where it announced plans to increase capital expenditures by a substantial $23 billion in 2025. Recently, GOOG also announced its intention to acquire cybersecurity firm Wiz Inc. for $32 billion, aiming to strengthen its position in the cloud computing market. The scale of these investments is substantial, but we note that GOOG generated $116 billion in operating profits during 2024 and ended the year with $93 billion of excess cash on its balance sheet. In our opinion, the increased capital expenditures are necessary to maintain GOOG’s position in its core search market. Beyond search, GOOG continues to build value across YouTube, Google Cloud, Waymo, and DeepMind, all of which leverage GOOG’s enviable infrastructure.
  • Our second-largest contractor in the quarter was American Express (AXP). AXP finished 2024 on a strong note, with earnings growing 23% for the year. While provisions for credit losses increased, reflecting a more cautious credit outlook, net write-off rates remained below historical averages, indicating strength in the underlying credit quality. AXP remains well-capitalized, and its affluent customer base is expected to fare better than the general economy in the coming months. However, the stock was not cheap, and we trimmed our position in Q1.

One thing that is common across both our top performers and contractors is that they are well-capitalized businesses with durable competitive positions in their respective markets. That is by design. Companies that operate from a position of strength are better positioned to navigate market downturns and often emerge in a stronger position as competitors go bankrupt or are acquired. Moreover, many of our portfolio companies actively repurchase their own stock. Lower stock prices allow them to repurchase a greater percentage of their outstanding shares, leaving remaining shareholders with a larger claim on future cashflows. This is a powerful and tax-free mechanism for long-term value creation.

New Purchase

We made one new purchase in the quarter: Icon PLC (ICLR). ICLR is a leading contract research organization ('CRO') based in Ireland that manages clinical trials for pharmaceutical and biotechnology companies worldwide. Despite some uncertainty driven by recent personnel changes at the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) and the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC), we believe that new drugs and medical devices will continue to be developed, and scientific progress will continue. New drugs require rigorous clinical testing before reaching the market. As a leading CRO, ICLR will capture its fair share of clinical trials once the dust settles.

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.
